stem: A builder designs a triangular roof truss where the angle at the peak is β and the angle at the base is α. These two angles are complementary, meaning α + β = 90°. The builder knows that cos β = 5/13. If the slanted edge of the roof (the hypotenuse of the right triangle) is 52 feet long, what is the height of the roof peak above the base, in feet?
explanation: Since α + β = 90°, the angles are complementary. By the complementary angle identity, sin α = cos β. Since cos β = 5/13, we know sin α = 5/13. The height of the roof peak is the side opposite angle α. Using sin α = opposite/hypotenuse: height = 52 × sin α = 52 × (5/13) = 260/13 = 20 feet.

distractorRationale.A: Extracts only the numerator of the ratio 5/13 without multiplying by the hypotenuse, treating 5 as the height = 5
distractorRationale.B: Correct: Recognizes sin α = cos β = 5/13 by the complementary angle identity, then computes height = 52 × (5/13) = 20
distractorRationale.C: Subtracts the height from the hypotenuse instead of recognizing it as the answer: computes 52 − 20 = 32
distractorRationale.D: Finds the horizontal base (adjacent to α) instead of the vertical height: uses cos α = 12/13, then computes 52 × (12/13) = 48

imageDescription: A triangular roof truss (right triangle) with the right angle at the base-wall junction. The angle at the base is labeled α, the angle at the peak is labeled β, and the slanted rafter (hypotenuse) is labeled 52 ft. The vertical height from the base to the peak is marked with a question mark. A note indicates α + β = 90°.
